summary: Joel Spolsky argues that every software team needs dedicated testers, recommending a ratio of one tester for every two programmers, with QA independent from the development team. He systematically dismantles five common excuses for not hiring testers: that bugs come from lazy programmers, that web software can be patched quickly, that customers can serve as testers, that qualified people don't want testing jobs, and that testers are too expensive. He uses Netscape's 'wide beta' strategy as a cautionary tale of how shipping untested software destroys a company's reputation. The post opens with an anecdote about journalist James Gleick, who excoriated Microsoft for buggy software but then denied bugs in his own product, illustrating how universal the problem of software quality is. Spolsky concludes that not hiring testers is a false economy since programmers forced into testing roles are far more expensive and less effective.
key_points: Bugs are inherent to software development, not a sign of programmer laziness — a second set of eyes with different habits catches what the original developer cannot
Netscape's 'wide beta' approach of using millions of customers as testers destroyed their reputation and produced unusable bug reports that nobody could process
The best testers are an order of magnitude more productive than average ones, but they tend to burn out and leave within 3-4 months, so teams must hire aggressively and continuously
QA should be independent from development with veto power over releases, and programmers forced into testing roles make poor testers while being far more expensive to replace
Not hiring testers is a false economy: testers cost far less than programmers, and the cost of losing a $100K programmer who got tired of doing testing work dwarfs multiple tester salaries
money_quotes: Bugs, by definition, leak out because programmers did not see the bug in their own code.
You are going to need 1 tester for every 2 programmers.
The QA department should be independent and powerful, it must not report to the development team, in fact, the head of QA should have veto power over releasing any software that doesn't meet muster.
With testers, like programmers, the best ones are an order of magnitude better than the average ones.
I'd rather have Jill on Mondays and Tuesdays than the rest of the QA team put together.
Programmers do not make good testers, and you'll lose a good programmer, who is a lot harder to replace.
Skimping on testers is such an outrageous false economy that I'm simply blown away that more people don't recognize it.

The whole point of letting your customers do the testing is that they find the bugs, and you fix them. Unfortunately, neither Netscape, nor any other company on earth, has the manpower to sift through bug reports from 2,000,000 customers.
key_insight: Not hiring dedicated testers is a false economy — testers are cheaper than programmers, catch bugs that developers structurally cannot see in their own code, and skipping QA either destroys your product's reputation or burns out expensive engineering talent on work they're poorly suited for.
